request.question: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?

title: 100 Classrooms program
Take the Lead
Yvonne Marceau
One World Youth Project
Potisarn Pittayakorn School
Pierre Dulaine
Mad Hot Ballroom
Index of Afghanistan-related articles
Theodore C. Marceau
2010 Panasonic Gobel Awards
sentences: The 100 Classrooms program was a joint project by Deutsche Welle and Cap Anamur to build and restore classrooms in Afghanistan after the U.S. invasion of 2001.
Beginning June 13, 2003, the program helped to build nearly 300 classrooms and 32 schools.
Take the Lead is a 2006 drama dance film directed by Liz Friedlander, and starring Antonio Banderas as dance instructor Pierre Dulaine, the founder of Dancing Classrooms.
It also stars Alfre Woodard, John Ortiz, Rob Brown, Yaya DaCosta, Dante Basco, Elijah Kelley, and Jenna Dewan.
The film was released on April 7, 2006.
Although based in New York City, it was filmed in Toronto.
Stock footage of various locations in New York City were used.
Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.
She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.
In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.
Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.
Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.
Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.

Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.
Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.
One World Youth Project (OWYP) is a non-profit 501(c)(3) corporation founded in Massachusetts and currently based in Washington DC.
The goal of One World Youth Project is to enhance education towards a more discerning, empathetic and empowered generation of global citizens.

In 2009, One World Youth Project launched the One World Hub program, a service-learning program that trains university students to lead a global education curriculum in local middle/high schools, and to connect these secondary school classrooms with partner classrooms in other countries.
The One World Youth Project global education curriculum includes the following units: cultural exchange, understanding of global challenges, community mapping, and service-learning.
Potisarn Pittayakorn is a coeducational school for grades 7 to 12 in Thailand.
Founded by Nuan Thamutaro (นวล ธมมธโร) in 1956, Potisarn is built on Mr. and Mrs. Saiputhong's land in an area of 6 rai 440 square wah.
When the school started in 1956, there was no classroom or any buildings.
Students had to study in a temple.
Later, Nuan Thamutaro raised funds for a school building.
The result was a 2-floor wooden building that contained 12 classrooms.
This building was finished in 1958.
In 1982, Potisarn School got funded for a 3-floor concrete building that contained 18 classrooms.
In 1983, the pool and toilet were built.
Later in 1984, the elementary education department selected Potisarn School into its elementary improvement program.
In this program, Potisarn's students got free books, free uniforms and free shelter.
In 1986, Suankularb Wittayalai recruited Potisarn to be their sub-school.
That meant any Potisarn students who met the requirements would be eligible to get into Suankalarb Wittayalai without any further exam.
Now the school consists of 3 main buildings, 1 medical building and 1 football field.
Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.
He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.
He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.
Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.
– 100 Classrooms program
Theodore Christopher Marceau (May 28, 1859 - June 22, 1922) was an American photographer.
He pioneered the creation of a national chain of photographic studios in the United States in the 1880s.
He founded the "Marceau Studios" in Manhattan, New York City, Philadelphia and Boston and was one of the most widely known photographers in the country.

The 13th Annual Panasonic Gobel Awards (or the 13th Annual Panasonic Awards) honoring the favorite in television programming/production works/individual, was held on March 26, 2010, at the Ballroom Theater Djakarta XXI in Jalan M.H. Thamrin, Menteng, Central Jakarta.
RCTI, Global TV and RCTI televised the ceremony in the Indonesia.
This 2010 ceremony awards this issue titled "Indonesia Unite".
The event coincides with the Golden Year of PT Panasonic Gobel Indonesia, as the organizer of Panasonic Awards.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Who besides Marceau founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program that was featured in a 2005 American documentary?
hop1_docs: Yvonne Marceau | Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.  She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.  In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.  Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.  Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.  Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.  Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.  Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.

Mad Hot Ballroom | Mad Hot Ballroom is a 2005 American documentary film directed and co-produced by Marilyn Agrelo and written and co-produced by Amy Sewell, about a ballroom dance program in the New York City Department of Education, the New York City public school system for fifth graders.  Several styles of dance are shown in the film, such as tango, foxtrot, swing, rumba and merengue.

Pierre Dulaine | Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.  He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.  He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Champion Ballroom Academy | The Champion Ballroom Academy (founded April 1990) is a dance studio in San Diego, California.  The studio's main specialties are social partner-dancing, competitive ballroom dance (aka.  Dancesport) and the Latin-dance-based aerobic program called Core Rhythms.

Warren Bullock | Warren Bullock is a professional ballroom dancer and dance teacher.  He owns a dance teaching business with a chain of 14 'studios', and also teaches on cruises.  He has won a number of awards as a dance teacher and coach and together with his wife Jane, pioneered the teaching of Ballroom and Latin American dancing in schools starting at Glebefields School in Tipton and King Edwards School for girls in Edgebaston before speaking and promoting this concept at the BDF conference in 1997 which resulted in dance becoming part of the national curriculum in some counties.  He was the main character in 8 one hour programmes of Baby Ballroom on channel 5Star - a reality documentary made by Firecracker Films based on Bullock, his family and Zig Zag Dance Factory <ref>http://www.channel5.com/show/baby-ballroom/ <ref>http://www.firecrackerfilms.com/broadcast/baby-ballroom/

Ballroom Bootcamp | Ballroom Bootcamp was an American reality television series that debuted on the TLC cable network, on October 7, 2005.  The series follows several individuals who try to improve their skill of dance.  The series ended on January 26, 2007, after two seasons.

Louis Van Amstel | Louis van Amstel (born June 23, 1972) is a Dutch-American ballroom dance champion, professional dancer, and choreographer who appears on the U.S. reality television series "Dancing with the Stars".  He is the creator of the popular dance fitness program LaBlast.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program / Mad Hot Ballroom (2005 documentary)

- From passage [1]: Yvonne Marceau "co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine" (1984) and "Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom."
- From passage [3]: "Pierre Dulaine ... founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children."
- No ambiguity in the passages: the only other named founder candidate is "Pierre Dulaine" (no alternative co-founders listed).
- Missing facts / bridge if more detail needed: exact founding date of the Dancing Classrooms program (not given); more on the program's institutional name variant ("Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms" vs. "Dancing Classrooms").

Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives:
1. No additional retrieval needed to identify the co-founder (passages explicitly name Pierre Dulaine) — defer final answer production to downstream component.
2. (If verification desired) Fetch the "Pierre Dulaine" page — inspect lede/infobox and sections "Dancing Classrooms" or "Career."
3. Fetch the "Yvonne Marceau" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Career" for corroboration.
4. Fetch "Mad Hot Ballroom" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Synopsis" to confirm program naming and film linkage.
hop2_docs: Pierre Dulaine | Pierre Dulaine (born 23 April 1944) is a dance instructor and dancer.  He invented the Dulaine method of teaching dance.  He also founded Dancing Classrooms, a Social and Emotional development program for 5th grade children that uses ballroom dancing as a vehicle to change the lives of the children and their families.

Take the Lead | Take the Lead is a 2006 drama dance film directed by Liz Friedlander, and starring Antonio Banderas as dance instructor Pierre Dulaine, the founder of Dancing Classrooms.  It also stars Alfre Woodard, John Ortiz, Rob Brown, Yaya DaCosta, Dante Basco, Elijah Kelley, and Jenna Dewan.  The film was released on April 7, 2006.  Although based in New York City, it was filmed in Toronto.  Stock footage of various locations in New York City were used.

Yvonne Marceau | Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.  She was born in Chicago, IL, and holds a B.F.A. from the University of Utah as well as an associate degree from the Imperial Society of Ballroom Dance.  In 1984, Marceau co-founded American Ballroom Theater with her dance partner, Pierre Dulaine.  Together, Marceau and Dulaine won numerous ballroom dance contests and were four-time winners of the British Exhibition ballroom competition.  Marceau is the recipient of various awards, including the Astaire Award and the Dance Magazine Award.  Along with Dulaine, Marceau co-founded the Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program, which is featured in the documentary Mad Hot Ballroom.  Marceau has been on the faculty at The Juilliard School since 1993 and has taught at the School of American Ballet as well as for numerous New York City social groups, including the Union Club and cotillion societies.  Marceau also currently teaches at the NYU Tisch New Studio on Broadway.

Jean-Pierre Duport | Jean-Pierre Duport (November 27, 1741 – December 31, 1818) was a cellist of the late 18th and early 19th centuries.  Along with his brother, Jean-Louis Duport (also a cellist), he was active in the musical life of France and Germany.  Jean-Pierre was the son of a dancing master, and a student of the founder of the French school of cello playing Martin Berteau (1700? –1771).

Monsieur Pierre | Monsieur Pierre, or simply Pierre, was the professional name of Pierre Jean Phillipe Zurcher-Margolle, (born Toulon, France – died London, 1963).  Pierre was a professional dancer and dance teacher: he was primarily responsible for introducing the Latin American dances to England, and for codifying them, and laying the groundwork for their use in competitions and in social dance.  The system he and his colleagues developed became the basis for all Latin and American competitions held under the World Dance Council (WDC).

Pierre Rameau | Pierre Rameau (1674 – 26 January 1748), was the French dancing master to Elisabetta Farnese, and the author of two books that now provide us with valuable information about Baroque dance.

Mandisi Arnold Shindo | Mandisi Arnold Shindo (born 1989, stage name Dr Disi) is a South African actor, writer, director, voice and dance performer, and the founder of the Iselwa Lesizwe Theatre Company.  He began pursuing his passion for theatre at age 11 in primary school, and since then has dedicated his life to the arts and to helping young people gain the skills for a tertiary level education.  He views the teaching of drama as "(his) calling and weapon to teach those who are not getting the formal classroom teaching."
response.summary_2: Pierre Dulaine co-founded Ballroom Theater’s Dancing Classrooms program with Yvonne Marceau. Pierre Dulaine is a dance instructor who founded Dancing Classrooms and devised the Dulaine method of teaching dance. Yvonne Marceau is an American ballroom dancer, choreographer, and instructor.
